Content:
Customer health scores are the astrology of customer success.

Both promise to predict the future using complex systems. Both give believers a sense of control over uncertain outcomes. Both consistently fail when it matters most.

The difference? When your horoscope is wrong, you lose nothing. When your health score is wrong, you lose customers.

94.8% of CS teams now use AI-powered health scoring. Platforms compete on algorithm sophistication. Leaders present colored dashboards as proof of predictive mastery.

Customers still churn without warning.

Your highest-value enterprise client scores 98% healthy. Usage is up. Support tickets down. Then they submit a 90-day cancellation notice.

The algorithm missed what a 15-minute phone call would have revealed: they've been evaluating competitors for six months.

Your CSMs already know which accounts are at risk. Trust their judgment over the algorithm's confidence.

I break down why health scores actively make teams worse at retention: https://churnisdead.com/newsletter/case-against-customer-health-scores

Strategy: Contrarian take with strong analogy that challenges industry orthodoxy, includes specific stats and real scenario, drives to newsletter

Content:
False positives in health scoring waste more resources than false negatives.

Mid-market accounts with 60% health scores get aggressive intervention campaigns. CSMs run discovery calls to identify expansion opportunities. Account managers get pulled into strategic conversations.

Meanwhile, the account was never at risk. They're seasonal users who go quiet every Q4. The health score algorithm doesn't know their business cycle.

False negatives create blind spots, but false positives create something worse: resource misallocation at scale.

When your team optimizes for metrics instead of relationships, three things happen:
• You waste time on healthy accounts
• You miss context that actually matters
• You train CSMs to game the system instead of serve customers

The best CSMs I know ignore the dashboard colors. They focus on conversation quality, stakeholder mapping, and business outcome alignment.

What do you rely on more: your health score or your gut feeling about an account?

#CustomerSuccess #CSM #SaaS
Strategy: Pure value post focusing on the resource waste angle, includes framework thinking, ends with engagement question

Content:
4 relationship triggers that matter more than health scores:

1. **Champion transition alerts**
Your internal advocate changes roles or companies. This matters more than any usage metric.

2. **Stakeholder mapping gaps**
You're only connected to one person in a multi-stakeholder decision. Relationship depth beats engagement frequency.

3. **Business outcome misalignment**
They're using your product, but not achieving their stated goals. Usage stats won't catch this disconnect.

4. **Expansion readiness signals**
Identifying growth opportunities within existing usage patterns. This prevents churn by building deeper partnerships.

These triggers focus on relationship strength rather than risk mitigation. They prevent churn by building stronger partnerships that survive contextual changes.

Health scores tell you everything except what you need to know.

It can't predict your champion just got a new job. It won't flag that your customer's biggest competitor acquired them.

Health scores track product engagement, support interactions, and meeting cadence. Meanwhile, the factors that drive 60% of enterprise churn remain invisible:

• Competitive displacement
• Company acquisitions
• Regulatory changes

These aren't edge cases. They're the majority.

Every CS platform vendor promises 60-90 day churn prediction accuracy. But they're measuring login frequency when the real decision happens in the C-suite.

What if we stopped trying to predict the future and started understanding what we can actually control?

Content:
The Churn Attribution Framework that replaced our health scores:

1. **CS-Preventable Churn**
   • Poor onboarding execution
   • Product adoption gaps
   • Relationship management failures

2. **Sales-Attributable Churn**
   • Wrong ICP qualification
   • Overpromised capabilities
   • Pricing misalignment

3. **Product-Related Churn**
   • Missing critical features
   • Performance issues
   • Integration failures

4. **Uncontrollable Churn**
   • Budget cuts and freezes
   • Strategic pivots
   • Company acquisitions

For 12 weeks, audit every churned account using these categories. Week 1-4: Data collection. Week 5-8: Pattern analysis. Week 9-12: Resource reallocation.

The goal isn't perfect attribution. It's resource allocation based on reality instead of algorithm confidence scores.

Stop spending time on prediction. Start investing in prevention.

Customer success happens when customers achieve outcomes without thinking about your product. When insights arrive exactly when they need them. When expansion surfaces automatically based on usage, not calendar reminders.

Content:
Every QBR invitation is a withdrawal from your relationship bank account.

When you ask customers to block time to hear you present their own data back to them, you're signaling that you don't understand their business.

When you schedule quarterly check-ins to discuss insights they could get on-demand, you're treating them like they need babysitting.

When you create PowerPoint presentations about their usage patterns, you're admitting your product doesn't surface intelligence automatically.

Customers notice this. They start seeing you as overhead instead of outcome.

The strongest customer relationships aren't maintained through meetings. They're built through continuous value delivery. Through systems that anticipate needs before customers realize they have them.

Through intelligence that arrives at the moment of decision, not the moment of scheduling.

Your competitors who figured this out aren't just winning deals. They're making your QBR invitations look desperate by comparison.

What would happen if you canceled all your QBRs next quarter and invested that time in building automated success experiences instead?

Strategy: Opens with concrete failure story that CS leaders recognize. Challenges conventional wisdom directly. Builds tension between vendor timeline vs customer reality. Strong CTA to full framework.

Content:
Your QBR format encourages performance theater instead of business decisions.

Traditional script: Health scores by segment. NRR trends. Win/loss analysis. Customer feedback themes. Action items for next quarter.

Teams spend weeks crafting narratives explaining why metrics moved. Building compelling explanations for trailing indicators while leading indicators go unexamined.

Executives nod along, ask clarifying questions about historical performance, then leave without making any forward-looking resource allocation decisions.

Meanwhile, the obstacles that will determine next quarter's results remain unaddressed. The resource gaps that will create churn stay unfilled. The strategic initiatives that could drive expansion never get prioritized.

The highest-leverage meeting in your CS organization becomes the least impactful.

Instead of asking 'What happened last quarter?' start asking 'What decisions do we need to make this month?'

Instead of reviewing trailing health scores, identify leading obstacle patterns.

Instead of explaining past performance, commit resources to future outcomes.

What's the biggest obstacle your customers face that your executive team could actually remove?

Content:
4 questions that turn QBR theater into business impact:

1. What obstacles are preventing customer outcomes this month?
Not: What were our health scores last quarter?

2. Which resource gaps will create churn if left unfilled?
Not: How did our retention metrics trend?

3. What strategic initiatives need executive support to drive expansion?
Not: What was our NRR performance analysis?

4. Which customer decisions are happening now that require our intervention?
Not: What themes emerged from customer feedback?

The shift is from historical reporting to forward-looking resource allocation.

From explaining what happened to deciding what happens next.

From trailing indicators to leading obstacles.

From performance analysis to business decisions.

Most CS leaders optimize their highest-leverage meeting for storytelling instead of problem-solving. The companies that make this transition optimize for business impact.

Content:
Your CFO just cut CS headcount 40% despite hitting every metric.

95% CSAT. 8.5 health score. 112% NRR.

What are you missing?

The brutal truth: You're being measured on financial models you've never seen.

While you optimize for customer happiness, your CFO calculates whether preventing $50K churn costs more than acquiring $50K new revenue.

While you celebrate 95% retention, they wonder why CS payroll exceeds the revenue you're saving.

Most CS teams think they cost $0.15-0.25 per retained dollar. CFOs calculate the real cost at $0.40-0.65 when they include benefits, tools, overhead, and opportunity cost.

Your CFO isn't anti-customer. They're pro-math.

Strategy: Leads with the shocking scenario that hooks CS leaders. Creates urgency and relatability. Positions the CFO as logical, not villainous, which makes the advice more palatable.

Content:
Strip out natural expansion and your 112% NRR looks very different.

Most CS teams celebrate 112% NRR. But break it down:

• 88% came from 2 enterprise accounts expanding regardless
• CS team of 12 managed routine check-ins for 400+ accounts
• Those 400 accounts renewed at exactly contract value
• Net CS-influenced growth: 2%
• CS cost as % of revenue: 8%

That's not sustainable math.

Your CFO sees this breakdown. You report the headline number.

The disconnect isn't that CFOs don't value customers. It's that CS metrics don't translate to financial impact.

What would change if you measured revenue-weighted retention instead of account-weighted retention?

Strategy: Pure value play that reframes a common CS metric. The math example is concrete and shocking. Ends with a thought-provoking question to drive engagement.

Content:
3 financial lenses your CFO uses to evaluate CS:

1. Unit Economics Reality
What's the fully-loaded cost to retain $1 of ARR? Include salary, benefits, tools, office space, management overhead, recruiting, training, and opportunity cost. Most CS teams think $0.15-0.25. Reality is $0.40-0.65.

2. Investment Payback Timeline
How long does CS headcount take to pay for itself? Not impact on retention rates, but when prevented churn revenue exceeds total cost of hire. CS leaders think quarterly. CFOs think 18-24 month payback periods.

3. Opportunity Cost Analysis
Every CS dollar is a dollar not spent on product, sales, or marketing. Would this CS investment generate more revenue as two AEs instead?

You see CS as essential. They see it as one option among many.

Full breakdown: https://churnisdead.com/newsletter/3-things-your-cfo-knows-about-cs

Content:
Your health score is 87% accurate at predicting churn. It's 0% effective at preventing it.

CS teams have become professional excuse-makers. We measure success by how rarely customers complain, not by how often they achieve their goals.

We optimized for customer comfort instead of customer outcomes. We sound like therapists in renewal meetings and wonder why conversations end with one-year contracts and seat reductions.

Meanwhile, AI is eliminating 70-80% of support tasks. Block cut 4,000 positions. Salesforce quietly reduced support from 9,000 to 5,000 people.

The teams surviving aren't the ones with the best health scores. They're the ones with courage to tell customers hard truths.

Customers don't need another vendor who makes them feel better about their problems. They need advisors who help them solve their problems.

The choice: Comfortable conversations that lead to churn, or courageous conversations that lead to outcomes.

Strategy: Direct challenge to CS orthodoxy with concrete examples and clear stakes. Links to newsletter with promise of solution.

Content:
Enterprise customers aren't paying you $200K to feel good about poor adoption.

They're paying you to ensure they get $2M in value.

Those are different jobs.

CS teams have confused being customer-centric with never making customers uncomfortable. So we create elaborate justification frameworks for why their ROI calculation is wrong, why their usage patterns are actually fine, and why their concerns aren't valid.

Meanwhile, their business metrics deteriorate. Their executive sponsor gets fired. Their budget gets cut. And they blame the economy.

The enterprise reality: Your customer's comfort today determines your renewal tomorrow.

But not in the way you think.

Comfortable conversations about uncomfortable metrics lead to churn. Uncomfortable conversations about comfortable metrics lead to expansion.

What uncomfortable conversation are you avoiding with your biggest customer?

#CustomerSuccess #SaaS #ChurnPrevention
Strategy: Reframes customer-centricity with concrete numbers. Ends with a reflection question to drive comments.

Content:
Three forces turned strategic advisors into professional excuse-makers:

1. The Support Team Mindset Migration
When companies moved support teams into CS, they imported the core principle: resolve immediate concerns with minimal escalation. Support optimizes for case closure. CS adopted this for conversation closure.

2. The Vendor-Customer Power Dynamic Myth
CS leaders teach that customers hold all the power in renewals. This creates a negotiating stance where CS argues from weakness instead of value.

3. The Metrics Misalignment
We measure customer satisfaction instead of customer progress. We celebrate CSMs who manage difficult conversations well instead of CSMs who eliminate the need for them.

The result: CS teams that predict churn perfectly but prevent it poorly.

The antidote: Courage-based conversations that focus on outcomes, not comfort.

Content:
I used to think being customer-centric meant never saying no.

I was wrong.

I spent years perfecting the art of gentle pushback. Finding diplomatic ways to avoid difficult conversations. Making customers feel heard without actually addressing their real problems.

My CSAT scores were perfect. My retention rates were terrible.

The breakthrough came when I realized my customers didn't need another vendor making them feel better about their problems. They needed an advisor helping them solve their problems.

The first time I told a customer their usage patterns would guarantee failure, they thanked me. Not immediately. But three months later when they hit their ROI targets.

Courage isn't confrontation. It's caring enough about their outcomes to risk their comfort.

The CS teams surviving the AI wave aren't the ones with sophisticated health scores. They're the ones willing to have conversations health scores can't automate.
